raw_case:
Recurrent splenic flexure colonic volvulus: A case report


Introduction and importance

Large-bowel obstruction (LBO) is an emergency condition that requires early identification and intervention. Among all causes of LBO colonic volvulus is the third leading cause worldwide. Colonic volvulus is an axial rotation of the colon around a fixed point. Splenic flexure volvulus is the least common location for colonic volvulus, accounting for <1 % of cases.

Case presentation

A 41-year-old male patient presented to the emergency department with a history of crampy abdominal pain, abdominal distension and failure to pass feces and flatus of three days duration. He had a history of laparotomy 1 year back, at which time de-rotation of the splenic flexure was done. This time, while preparing the patient for emergency laparotomy, he passed both feces and flatus. On the same admission, he was operated and left hemicolectomy and end to end anastomosis was done.

Clinical discussion

Due to splenic flexure attachments to the left upper quadrant via Splenic ligament splenic flexure colonic volvulus is very rare. Risk factors include congenitally absent ligaments, congenital bands, acquired adhesions, previous colonic surgery. With the appropriate clinical setting radiographic diagnosis is suggested when there is a markedly dilated, air-filled colon with an abrupt termination at the anatomic splenic flexure, two widely separated air- fluid levels, and an empty descending and sigmoid colon.

Conclusion

Following adequate resuscitation urgent exploratory laparotomy is recommended in splenic flexure volvulus. If the clinical condition of the patient allows colonic resection with continuity restoration is the preferred conventional approach.

Highlights

•Splenic flexure volvulus is the least common location for colonic volvulus, accounting for <1% of cases•Risk factors include congenitally absent ligaments, congenital bands, acquired adhesions, previous colonic surgery.•With the appropriate clinical setting radiographic diagnosis could be suggested.•Colonic resection with continuity restoration is the preferred conventional approach.•The alternatives to resection procedures are detorsion, colopexy, and colostomy.1

Introduction

Large-bowel obstruction (LBO) is an emergency condition that requires early identification and intervention. It could be a mechanical obstruction, adynamic ileus or a colonic pseudo-obstruction [1]. The causes for mechanical colonic obstruction include colo-rectal cancer, colonic volvulus, Colonic strictures, complicated diverticular disease and intussusceptions. Colonic volvulus is the third leading cause of large bowel obstruction worldwide, following colorectal cancer and complicated sigmoid diverticulitis [2]. It occurs when there is an axial rotation of an air-filled segment of the colon along its mesentery. All mobile segments of the colon could be affected by volvulus [3]. Sigmoid colon is involved in 60–75 % of cases, cecum in 25–40 % of cases, transverse colon in 1–4 % of cases and splenic flexure in 1 % of cases [2,3]. Leading causes of colonic obstruction vary by region. In the “volvulus belt” countries an endemic area that includes Africa, South America, Russia, Eastern Europe, the Middle East, India and Brazil, colonic volvulus represents 13 to 42 % of all intestinal obstructions [2]. In Ethiopia sigmoid volvulus was the most common cause of large intestine obstruction, followed by colonic cancer [4]. Etiologies for colonic volvulus include chronic constipation, high fiber diet, frequent use of laxatives, history of laparotomy and congenital anatomic predispositions [2,3].Clinical presentation of colonic volvulus varies by site of colonic volvulus and the geographical region. In the “volvulus belt” countries, volvulus usually occurs in young men while in Western countries, volvulus preferentially affects elderly males. Patients with colonic volvulus have nonspecific symptoms, combining in varying degrees of intermittent episodes of abdominal distension, crampy abdominal pain, constipation, nausea and vomiting. These episodes may spontaneously resolve and then recur or patients may have acute fulminant clinical presentation with peritonitis and shock [2,3].The normal splenic flexure is attached to the left upper quadrant via the gastrocolic ligament, the phrenocolic ligament, and the splenocolic ligament. Due to this anatomic fixation splenic flexure colonic volvulus is very rare. <50 cases of splenic flexure volvulus are reported in English literature since the first case was reported by Buenger in 1954 [3,5,6]. The most common patient presentation with splenic flexure volvulus is acute large bowel obstruction, despite reports of chronic constipation in the literature [3,7,8].Unless clinicians have high level of suspicion as a result of nonspecific symptom splenic flexure volvulus is misdiagnosed for other causes of colonic obstruction which will delay management. In the development of this case report we used SCARE guideline [9].2

Case report

A 41-year-old male patient presented to the emergency department with a history of intermittent crampy abdominal pain of three days duration which was associated with abdominal distension, failure to pass of both feces and flatus of the same day duration. He had a history of self-limited intermittent crampy abdominal pain and abdominal distension which relives when he passes flatus for the past 11 months. One year prior to the current admission he was presented with crampy abdominal pain that was associated with abdominal distension, and failure to pass both feces and flatus for which rectal tube defilation was tried but was not successful. He was taken to the operating theater and explored through midline vertical laparotomy incision. The intraoperative finding was redundant viable splenic flexure which rotates 270 degrees along its mesentery. The splenic flexure was mobile with lacking phrenocolic ligament, and splenocolic ligament. Distal part of the transverse colon was dilated and involved in the volvulus. The operating team did derotation of the volvulated colon without colpopexy or proper colonic resection.On current presentation, the patient was acutely sick looking in pain, and his vitals were blood pressure of 124/76 mm Hg (mmHg), pulse rate of 84 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 22 breaths per minute, and temperature of 36.2 degrees Celsius. He had a slightly dry tongue and buccal mucosa. The abdomen was distended with visible peristalsis, there was midline laparotomy scar, and the bowel sounds were hyperactive. There was no superficial or deep tenderness and upon percussion there was hyper tympanic note. On digital rectal examination there was soft stool but no mass or blood on examining finger. With an impression of large bowel obstruction secondary to recurrent colonic volvulus, he was kept NPO, resuscitated with 2 l of normal saline and produced adequate urine output.He was investigated with complete blood count (CBC), blood group, cross match and erect abdominal X-ray (erect AXR). CBC showed normal cell parameters while erect AXR showed distended bowel with large bowel caliber and few air fluid levels and paucity of air in the rectum which suggests large bowel obstruction. No air under right hemidiaphragm (Fig. 1).With the same assessment the patient was prepared for emergency exploratory laparotomy and while he was being admitted to the ward, he passed stool and flatus with complete cessation of the pain and distention. He then was kept in the ward where bowel preparation was done and operated with the same admission after 1 week. He was explored through midline laparotomy and the intra operative finding was hugely dilated redundant splenic flexure of colon (Fig. 2). There is also no attachment of the splenic flexure to the posterior abdominal wall (Fig. 3) Ecchymosis and thickening of the peritoneum over splenic flexure mesentery (Fig. 3, labeled with the green arrow). The Cecum and appendix were in their normal anatomic position which is the right lower quadrant and no redundant sigmoid colon. There was no adhesion between the bowels or to the abdominal wall.The descending colon mobilized and left hemicolectomy was done; hand sewed colo-colic anastomosis was done in two layers with vicarly 2.0. Post operatively we put him on Intravenous ceftriaxone, metronidazole, tramadol and diclofenac. He started sips after 24 h of surgery which was progressively advanced to full feeding and stayed in the hospital for 6 days. On sixth post-operative day he was discharged home with per oral antibiotic (Amoxicillin Clavulanic acid) and paracetamol, appointed to the surgical post-operative clinic for follow up after one week. His follow up on his 1st week, 1st month and 3rd month post operation were smooth with Clavien-Dindo classification (CDC) class one postoperative morbidity [10,11].3

Discussion

Colonic volvulus occurs when there is an axial rotation of an air-filled segment of the colon along its mesentery. It is the third leading cause of large bowel obstruction worldwide, following colorectal cancer and complicated sigmoid diverticulitis [2]. All mobile segments of the colon could be affected by volvulus [3]. Sigmoid colon is involved in 60–75 % of cases, cecum in 25–40 % of cases, transverse colon in 1–4 % of cases and splenic flexure in 1 % of cases [2]. Due to splenic flexure attachments to the left upper quadrant via the gastrocolic ligament, the phrenocolic ligament, and the splenocolic ligament splenic flexure colonic volvulus is very rare. Risk factors for splenic flexure volvulus include congenitally absent ligaments of the splenic flexure especially phrenocolic ligament, congenital bands, acquired adhesions, previous colonic surgery involving ligaments of the splenic flexure [12], chronic constipation, or neurologic and psychiatric disease [5,13,14]. Despite the fact that he had previously undergone surgery, there was no significant adhesion. Given the prior surgery finding of a redundant viable splenic flexure that rotated 270 degrees along its mesentery and a mobile splenic flexure with no phrenocolic or splenocolic ligaments. In our situation, the most likely cause of splenic flexure volvulus was the congenital absence of splenic flexure ligaments.Clinical presentation of splenic flexure volvulus could be either acute or chronic. Acute presentation is with crampy abdominal pain, abdominal distension and/or failure to pass of feces and/or flatus is the commonest presentation. It may be with or without sign and symptoms of bowel ischemia [7,15,16]. Chronic presentation with history of self-limited intermittent crampy abdominal pain and abdominal distension and constipation is the next common clinical presentation of splenic flexure volvulus [8].Radiographic diagnosis of a splenic flexure volvulus is suggested in the appropriate clinical setting when the following are seen: (a) a markedly dilated, air-filled colon with an abrupt termination at the anatomic splenic flexure; (b) two widely separated air- fluid levels, one of which is in the transverse colon and the other in the cecum; (c) an empty descending and sigmoid colon; and (d) a characteristic beak at the anatomic splenic flexure at a barium enema examination [6]. Our case had an erect abdominal x-ray which showed a markedly dilated, air-filled colon with an abrupt termination at the anatomic splenic flexure with two widely separated air- fluid levels, one of which is in the transverse colon and the other in the cecum and paucity of air in the rectum which suggests splenic flexure volvulus (Fig. 1). With the existing previous surgical history with an intraoperative finding of mobile redundant splenic flexure our top diagnosis for the current presentation was recurrent splenic flexure volvulus. The presence of no subjective fever, normal heart rate, normal temperature, no abdominal tenderness, and no air under right hemi diaphragm leads us to consider viable splenic flexure volvulus. Though we didn't do it in our patient due to recourse limitation cross sectional imaging like abdominal CT scan and barium enema examination are helpful in the workup of such cases. Synchronous and metachronous volvulus could happen in the presence of splenic flexure volvulus, as a result of this potential sites of volvulus should be checked, like sigmoid colon or the cecum [17,18].The initial management of such cases should start with adequate goal directed resuscitation as they are likely to have third spacing of the body fluid. On initial evaluation our patient had signs of dehydration which was corrected by two bags of normal saline. It is reported that posture advice, including the knee–chest position and abdominal compression using colonic intraluminal pressure for the resolution of incomplete volvulus during pain attacks is effective [19]. Spontaneous resolution of symptom in our case could be due to unintentional postural changes during patient admission for an emergency surgery. Splenic flexure volvulus due to its rarity and difficulties in diagnosis often lead to delays in management with a high mortality rate of 33 %. Simple detorsion has no place since it is associated with a significant recurrence rate and a high mortality [2,20]. In patients with clinically viable splenic flexure volvulus endoscopic derotation followed by elective colectomy is the preferred management. While in patients with clinically suspected gangrenous splenic flexure volvulus urgent exploratory laparotomy is the standard of care.The choice of surgical treatment remains debatable due to a lack of randomized control trials, if the clinical condition of the patient allows colonic resection with colonic end to end anastomosis is the preferred conventional approach. The alternatives to resection procedures are detorsion, colopexy, and colostomy. To choose among these options the surgeon should depend on presence or absence of risk factors of increased morbidity like whether the indication for surgery is an emergency surgery, age > 70 years, malnutrition and neurological impairment [2]. If detorsion is done as an emergency procedure, clinicians should recommend their patients undergo elective surgery, as SFV is associated with a high recurrence rate. [2].4

Conclusion

Following adequate resuscitation urgent intervention is recommended in splenic flexure volvulus. The intervention could be endoscopic derotation followed by elective colectomy especially in patients with no signs of bowel infraction at presentation. Exploratory laparotomy is recommended in patients with clinically suspected gangrenous splenic flexure volvulus or if endoscopic intervention is not readily available. If the clinical condition of the patient allows colonic resection with colonic end to end anastomosis is the preferred conventional approach.

Guarantor

Alemneh Mitku Chekol, M.D

Research registration number

Registration of research studies is not applicable for our work.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Alemneh Mitku Chekol, M.D. – Primary operating surgeon, Study concept and design, writing the paper and literature review.Degefa Tadesse Alemu, M.D. – Involved in the patient management, acquisition of data and critical review of the paper.Tibebu Geremew Haile, M.D. – Involved in the patient management, acquisition of data and critical review of the paper.Bedru Areb Kedir M.D. – Involved in patient management, acquisition of data, and critical review of the paper.Dawit Dereje leuleberehan, M.D. – Involved in acquisition of data, and critical review of the paper.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient, in their native language, for publication of non-identifying information including accompanying intraoperative images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

Ethical approval is deemed unnecessary by the hospital ethical committee as this is a single case encountered during practice and it doesn't involve human or animal experiments.

Funding

No funding was provided for this case report.

Declaration of competing interest

No conflict of interest between the authors or any financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that could inappropriately influence this work.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 41-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Intermittent crampy abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and inability to pass feces and flatus for 3 days
- **History of Present Illness:** Crampy abdominal pain and distension recurrent over 11 months; history of laparotomy one year prior for a similar presentation, during which de-rotation of a 270-degree splenic flexure volvulus (redundant and mobile splenic flexure without ligaments) was performed without colopexy or resection.
- **Past Medical History:** Previous laparotomy for splenic flexure volvulus
- **Personal History:** None
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Acutely sick: distended abdomen with visible peristalsis, hyper tympanic note on percussion, no tenderness, normal vitals (BP 124/76 mmHg, pulse 84/min, RR 22/min, temp 36.2°C); hyperactive bowel sounds.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Complete blood count (CBC) within normal limits.
  - Erect abdominal X-ray: Markedly dilated colon abruptly terminating at the splenic flexure, with air-fluid levels and paucity of air in the rectum indicative of large-bowel obstruction. No free air under diaphragm.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial suspicion based on presentation of large bowel obstruction (LBO):** Several causes of mechanical LBO were considered, including colorectal cancer, sigmoid volvulus, cecal volvulus, and recurrent colonic volvulus.
   - **Colorectal cancer or diverticular disease:** Less likely due to lack of systemic signs of malignancy (e.g., weight loss) or local inflammatory features.
   - **Sigmoid or cecal volvulus:** Excluded by imaging and the prior history of splenic flexure volvulus.
   - **Recurrent splenic flexure volvulus:** Strongly supported by imaging findings (dilated colon with abrupt termination at splenic flexure) and prior operative history (absence of colonic ligaments, mobile redundant splenic flexure).

2. **Evaluation of viability of bowel:**
   - Absence of fever, tachycardia, abdominal tenderness, or pneumoperitoneum supported a viable bowel condition.

3. **Role of imaging and prior surgical findings:**
   - Imaging revealed hallmark features of splenic flexure volvulus.
   - Surgical findings during prior laparotomy (mobile, ligament-less, redundant splenic flexure with viable colon) pointed toward a congenital predisposition.

4. **Final confirmation:**
   - Spontaneous resolution of symptoms supported a mechanical obstruction due to recurrent splenic flexure volvulus. Dehydration corrected via fluid resuscitation.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Recurrent splenic flexure volvulus secondary to congenital absence of splenic flexure ligaments, treated with left hemicolectomy and primary colonic anastomosis.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 41-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Intermittent crampy abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and inability to pass feces and flatus for 3 days
- **History of Present Illness:** Crampy abdominal pain and distension recurrent over 11 months; history of laparotomy one year prior for a similar presentation, during which de-rotation of a 270-degree splenic flexure volvulus (redundant and mobile splenic flexure without ligaments) was performed without colopexy or resection.
- **Past Medical History:** Previous laparotomy for splenic flexure volvulus
- **Personal History:** None
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Acutely sick: distended abdomen with visible peristalsis, hyper tympanic note on percussion, no tenderness, normal vitals (BP 124/76 mmHg, pulse 84/min, RR 22/min, temp 36.2°C); hyperactive bowel sounds.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Complete blood count (CBC) within normal limits.
  - Erect abdominal X-ray: Markedly dilated colon abruptly terminating at the splenic flexure, with air-fluid levels and paucity of air in the rectum indicative of large-bowel obstruction. No free air under diaphragm.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```

[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's history. He's a 41-year-old male with a chief complaint of intermittent crampy abdominal pain, distension, and inability to pass feces or flatus for three days. But he also has a history of similar symptoms over the past 11 months, and a previous laparotomy a year ago for a splenic flexure volvulus. The surgery involved de-rotation without colopexy or resection. 

So, the key points here are recurrent symptoms and prior surgery for volvulus. Volvulus is a twisting of the intestine, which can cause obstruction. Since he had a previous surgery for this, it's possible that the same issue is recurring. The fact that the surgery didn't include colopexy (which is a procedure to fix the position of the colon) or resection (removing part of the colon) might mean that the underlying issue hasn't been fully addressed, leading to a recurrence.

Now, looking at the current symptoms: he's having obstruction again. The physical exam shows a distended abdomen with visible peristalsis, hyperactive bowel sounds, and no tenderness. The X-ray shows a markedly dilated colon ending at the splenic flexure with air-fluid levels and no air in the rectum, which is classic for large bowel obstruction. Also, no free air under the diaphragm, so no perforation.

The previous surgery was for a 270-degree volvulus of the splenic flexure. Since he's had this before, the recurrence is likely due to the same anatomical issue. The fact that the colon was redundant and mobile without ligaments might make it prone to twisting again. Without colopexy, the colon remains mobile, increasing the risk of volvulus recurrence.

So, putting it all together: the patient's current presentation with obstruction symptoms, the X-ray findings, and his history of prior splenic flexure volvulus that wasn't fixed with colopexy or resection, points to a recurrence of splenic flexure volvulus causing large bowel obstruction. The absence of tenderness and free air might suggest that it's not yet a perforated bowel, but the obstruction is significant. The hyperactive bowel sounds could be due to the body's attempt to move contents past the obstruction, which is common in early stages of bowel obstruction.
</think>

### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with acute symptoms of bowel obstruction (abdominal distension, inability to pass flatus or feces, hyperactive bowel sounds) consistent with a mechanical obstruction, likely involving the large bowel given the location of the prior surgery (splenic flexure).  
<step 2> His history of a prior laparotomy for splenic flexure volvulus (without colopexy or resection) suggests a predisposition to recurrent volvulus due to redundant, mobile splenic flexure colon. Recurrent volvulus is a well-recognized complication in such cases.  
<step 3> The erect abdominal X-ray findings (dilated colon terminating at the splenic flexure with air-fluid levels and absence of rectal air) confirm large-bowel obstruction localized to the left colon, consistent with splenic flexure volvulus.  
<step 4> The absence of free air excludes perforation, and the lack of tenderness suggests the obstruction is not yet complicated by ischemia or perforation.  
<step 5> The chronic history of intermittent symptoms over 11 months aligns with recurrent episodes of volvulus, which are more likely to occur in patients with prior untreated or inadequately fixed splenic flexure volvulus.  

### Answer:
Recurrent splenic flexure volvulus causing large-bowel obstruction.
